library.title: Top 50 Rotational Athletes and Rotational Power Exercise Library
library.purpose: Build rotational athletes who can access rotation, control rotation, produce force through the ground and trunk, express high-quality rotational power, and brake or redirect rotational momentum safely.
library.source_basis: Primary authoring source used: exercise_card_details_for_llm.md. It defines the Vortex Card v2 workflow, phase-first authoring, canonical phase keys, taxonomy tags using key + weight, publish gates, authoring JSON format, movement requirements, coaching execution, dosage, scaling, safety, regimen, phaseProfile, and common authoring mistakes. Purpose.txt was requested and searched for, but a file literally titled Purpose.txt was not located in the accessible File Library results for this run. This deliverable therefore preserves that caveat and applies the available Card v2 guide, existing Vortex card conventions, and stable coaching expertise for rotational athletes and rotational power.

selection_principles.application: Each exercise must support hip-to-trunk-to-shoulder force transfer for athletes who rotate, throw, swing, strike, cut, or brace against contact.
Mobility and sequencing stay low fatigue; rotational power lives in Output while fresh; strength belongs in Capacity; anti-rotation and braking live in Resilience.
The best options teach rotation through feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders without chasing loaded lumbar twisting under fatigue.
High-intent throws, jumps, and reactive cuts are low volume with full rest; once speed, accuracy, posture, or landing quality drops, the set ends.
Each exercise has clear setup, quality gate, scaling path, and safety stop signs for youth, teens, adults, older adults, and pregnancy/postpartum considerations.

categories.title: Rotational Access, Hip-Shoulder Separation, and Sequencing
categories.focus: Low-fatigue drills that open thoracic rotation, hip internal/external rotation, lateral weight shift, and trunk-pelvis timing before power work.

categories.title: Anti-Rotation, Bracing, and Rotational Braking
categories.focus: Control drills that teach athletes to resist unwanted turn, stabilize the pelvis and ribcage, and brake rotational force before expressing power.

categories.title: Rotational Strength and Force Transfer
categories.focus: Loaded strength patterns that build hips, trunk, shoulders, and unilateral/frontal-plane control so rotational power has a stronger base.

categories.title: Medicine-Ball Rotational Power Output
categories.focus: High-intent throws that train rapid hip-to-trunk sequencing, bracing, release timing, and safe deceleration while the athlete is fresh.

categories.title: Rotational Speed, Elasticity, and Change-of-Direction Integration
categories.focus: Footwork, jumps, bounds, pivots, and cuts that connect rotation to sport movement, reorientation, braking, and re-acceleration.

library.programming_rule: Rotational power is quality-first. Access and sequencing can happen early at low fatigue; high-intent throws, jumps, and cuts belong in Output while fresh; loaded rotational strength belongs in Capacity; anti-rotation and braking belong in Resilience. Stop before speed, accuracy, posture, landing, or trunk control degrades.

cards.name: 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach
cards.category: Rotational Access, Hip-Shoulder Separation, and Sequencing
cards.cardSummary: 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach is a Prepare & Access exercise for hip internal/external rotation and ribcage-pelvis organization. Use it when the goal is rotational skill, force transfer, or power quality rather than fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after a brief temperature raise and before Movement Intelligence, Output, Capacity, or sport skill work.
cards.description: Perform 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach slowly enough to create usable rotational access and coordination. Move through pain-free range, breathe, and own the position before adding speed or load.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: mobilize. Coach 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach as rotational-athlete work: rotation should be organized through feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, shoulders, and the implement or direction change - not forced through the low back.
cards.athleteLanguage: Open the range, feel the turn, and do not force your low back.
cards.selectionRationale: Gives rotational athletes usable hip rotation and trunk reach without speed, load, or fatigue.
cards.dominantIntent: Hip internal/external rotation and ribcage-pelvis organization
cards.whyItWorks: Gives rotational athletes usable hip rotation and trunk reach without speed, load, or fatigue. Access drills work because they reduce positional restriction and improve the athlete's ability to rotate through the right joints before adding intensity.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Prepare & Access because it creates rotational range, activation, and readiness without meaningful fatigue.
cards.commonMisuse: Turning rotational access work into forced end-range stretching, speed work, or extra fatigue before the athlete owns clean range.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress by range, rhythm, stance, or sport-specific angle. Regress to smaller range, slower tempo, more support, and fewer coordination demands.

rotational_power_profile.rotation_source: feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders; avoid forced lumbar twisting
rotational_power_profile.sequence_priority: load or gather, brace, rotate, transfer, finish, and reset
rotational_power_profile.quality_rule: The rep counts only if the athlete keeps foot pressure, rib-pelvis organization, clean rotational path, safe finish, and a reset that could be repeated.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach slowly enough to create usable rotational access and coordination. Move through pain-free range, breathe, and own the position before adding speed or load.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.
Coach one clean practice rep before adding load, speed, partner input, or decision demand.
execution_steps: Move into the starting position slowly and find the target joint or stance position.
Rotate, reach, shift, or step through pain-free range while breathing naturally.
Return with control and repeat only as long as the motion improves readiness without fatigue.
coach_cues: Rotate from the ground up.
Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Open range without forcing it.
Smooth first, then crisp.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if the athlete keeps foot pressure, rib-pelvis organization, clean rotational path, safe finish, and a reset that could be repeated.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.

dosage.notes: 1-2 sets x 5-8 easy reps per side; keep it access-focused.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use playful, slow, low-range reps for 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach; stop before the athlete turns mobility or sequencing into a race.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Keep 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach crisp and low dose. Add one constraint only after the athlete can repeat posture, breathing, and safe finish.
scaling.teen: Use 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach as part of a rotational development progression. Keep quality higher than volume and avoid fatigue-based twisting.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the lowest-risk version of 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach. Reduce range, speed, stance challenge, and coordination demand before adding complexity.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach by adding smoother rhythm, slightly larger range, or more sport-specific stance without fatigue.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, smaller range, lower speed, and easier exits for 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach. Avoid dizziness, aggressive twisting, or fast direction changes unless already trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance when needed. Avoid breath-holding, abdominal pressure symptoms, pelvic symptoms, dizziness, aggressive trunk rotation, and positions that feel unsafe during 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by symptoms, training age, body size, rotation history, pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure symptoms, sport demand, and current readiness.
doNotUseWhen: The athlete has pain, dizziness, neurological symptoms, or medical restrictions that make rotation unsafe.
The athlete cannot control basic stance, brace, or landing positions for the chosen variation.
The session has already created enough fatigue that rotation quality or decision quality is degraded.

readiness_checks: Athlete can rotate through pain-free range without pinching or guarding.
Athlete can brace and breathe without breath holding or rib flare.
Athlete understands the start, finish, reset, and stop command.
contraindications: Current spine, hip, shoulder, knee, or ankle pain aggravated by rotation.
Uncontrolled dizziness, balance loss, or symptoms during turning.
Inability to follow spacing, throw, landing, or stop commands.

mediaReferences: Internal demo needed: front, side, and rotation-angle view.
Use sport-coach review for throwing, hitting, swinging, or cutting transfer examples.
mediaInternalNotes: Film first set from front and side. Approve progression only when rotation source, finish, and reset are repeatable.

cards.name: Standing Hip-Shoulder Separation Drill
cards.category: Rotational Access, Hip-Shoulder Separation, and Sequencing
cards.cardSummary: Standing Hip-Shoulder Separation Drill is a Movement Intelligence exercise for pelvis and ribcage dissociation. Use it when the goal is rotational skill, force transfer, or power quality rather than fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after warm-up while the nervous system is fresh, before high-intent Output, heavy strength, or conditioning.
cards.description: Perform Standing Hip-Shoulder Separation Drill slowly enough to create usable rotational access and coordination. Move through pain-free range, breathe, and own the position before adding speed or load.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: coordinate. Coach Standing Hip-Shoulder Separation Drill as rotational-athlete work: rotation should be organized through feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, shoulders, and the implement or direction change - not forced through the low back.
cards.athleteLanguage: Open the range, feel the turn, and do not force your low back.
cards.selectionRationale: Teaches the athlete to initiate rotation from the ground and hips while the trunk stays organized instead of spinning as one stiff block.
cards.whyItWorks: Teaches the athlete to initiate rotation from the ground and hips while the trunk stays organized instead of spinning as one stiff block. The drill improves timing and body map quality while the athlete is fresh, so later speed or power work has a cleaner movement answer.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Movement Intelligence because the main adaptation is coordination, rhythm, shape control, and movement literacy while fresh.
cards.commonMisuse: Rushing reps, adding too many cues, or letting rhythm and posture break while pretending more chaos equals better athleticism.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress by range, rhythm, stance, or sport-specific angle. Regress to smaller range, slower tempo, more support, and fewer coordination demands.

cards.name: Side Plank Row

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Side Plank Row as a control drill. Create or resist rotational force while breathing, keeping ribs and pelvis organized, and stopping before compensation appears.

cards.description: Perform Landmine Rotation as controlled loaded strength work. Use load, stance, and range that allow clean bracing, hip contribution, and repeatable rotational force transfer.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: rotational_force_transfer_strength. Coach Landmine Rotation as rotational-athlete work: rotation should be organized through feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, shoulders, and the implement or direction change - not forced through the low back.
cards.athleteLanguage: Brace first, move the load through your hips and trunk, and keep every rep repeatable.
cards.selectionRationale: Creates a progressive rotational loading problem with a guided arc that is easier to coach than free twisting under load.
cards.whyItWorks: Creates a progressive rotational loading problem with a guided arc that is easier to coach than free twisting under load. Strength work gives the athlete more force and tissue reserve to express rotation later, but the load must stay controlled enough that the hips and trunk transfer force instead of the low back twisting under strain.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is progressive loaded strength, force production, tissue capacity, and structural reserve for rotational actions.
cards.commonMisuse: Loading rotation before the athlete can brace, hinge, lunge, or control the rib-pelvis relationship; do not chase heavy twisting volume.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress by load, range, stance challenge, tempo, or asymmetry. Regress by reducing load, lever length, band/cable tension, or rotational amplitude.

rotational_power_profile.rotation_source: feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders; avoid forced lumbar twisting
rotational_power_profile.sequence_priority: load or gather, brace, rotate, transfer, finish, and reset
rotational_power_profile.quality_rule: The rep counts only if the athlete keeps foot pressure, rib-pelvis organization, clean rotational path, safe finish, and a reset that could be repeated.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Landmine Rotation as controlled loaded strength work. Use load, stance, and range that allow clean bracing, hip contribution, and repeatable rotational force transfer.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.
Select a load the athlete can control without lumbar twisting, breath holding, or loss of stance.
Coach one clean practice rep before adding load, speed, partner input, or decision demand.
execution_steps: Set stance, grip, and rib-pelvis position before moving the load.
Move through the planned range while the hips and trunk share the rotational or diagonal force demand.
Return under control and stop the set before load path, brace, or posture changes.
coach_cues: Rotate from the ground up.
Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Load the hips, not the spine.
Control the return as much as the drive.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
Load is progressed before the athlete controls the rotational path.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if the athlete keeps foot pressure, rib-pelvis organization, clean rotational path, safe finish, and a reset that could be repeated.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.

dosage.notes: 2-4 sets x 5-8 controlled reps each side where applicable.

scaling.youth_beginner: Teach the pattern of Landmine Rotation with no load or light load, emphasizing stance, brace, and controlled rotation.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Keep Landmine Rotation crisp and low dose. Add one constraint only after the athlete can repeat posture, breathing, and safe finish.
scaling.teen: Use Landmine Rotation as part of a rotational development progression. Keep quality higher than volume and avoid fatigue-based twisting.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the lowest-risk version of Landmine Rotation. Reduce load, range, lever length, tempo difficulty, and asymmetry before adding resistance.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress Landmine Rotation with load, range, intent, or unilateral demand while keeping clean rib-pelvis alignment.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, smaller range, lower speed, and easier exits for Landmine Rotation. Avoid dizziness, aggressive twisting, or fast direction changes unless already trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance when needed. Avoid breath-holding, abdominal pressure symptoms, pelvic symptoms, dizziness, aggressive trunk rotation, and positions that feel unsafe during Landmine Rotation.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by symptoms, training age, body size, rotation history, pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure symptoms, sport demand, and current readiness.
doNotUseWhen: The athlete has pain, dizziness, neurological symptoms, or medical restrictions that make rotation unsafe.
The athlete cannot control basic stance, brace, or landing positions for the chosen variation.
The session has already created enough fatigue that rotation quality or decision quality is degraded.

readiness_checks: Athlete can rotate through pain-free range without pinching or guarding.
Athlete can brace and breathe without breath holding or rib flare.
Athlete understands the start, finish, reset, and stop command.
contraindications: Current spine, hip, shoulder, knee, or ankle pain aggravated by rotation.
Uncontrolled dizziness, balance loss, or symptoms during turning.
Inability to follow spacing, throw, landing, or stop commands.

mediaReferences: Internal demo needed: front, side, and rotation-angle view.
Use sport-coach review for throwing, hitting, swinging, or cutting transfer examples.
mediaInternalNotes: Film first set from front and side. Approve progression only when rotation source, finish, and reset are repeatable.

Strength work gives the athlete more force and tissue reserve to express rotation later, but the load must stay controlled enough that the hips and trunk transfer force instead of the low back twisting under strain.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is progressive loaded strength, force production, tissue capacity, and structural reserve for rotational actions.
cards.commonMisuse: Loading rotation before the athlete can brace, hinge, lunge, or control the rib-pelvis relationship; do not chase heavy twisting volume.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress by load, range, stance challenge, tempo, or asymmetry. Regress by reducing load, lever length, band/cable tension, or rotational amplitude.

rotational_power_profile.rotation_source: feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders; avoid forced lumbar twisting
rotational_power_profile.sequence_priority: load or gather, brace, rotate, transfer, finish, and reset
rotational_power_profile.quality_rule: The rep counts only if the athlete keeps foot pressure, rib-pelvis organization, clean rotational path, safe finish, and a reset that could be repeated.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Cable Shot-Put Press as controlled loaded strength work. Use load, stance, and range that allow clean bracing, hip contribution, and repeatable rotational force transfer.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.
Set band or cable height to match the intended line of pull and keep tension controllable.
Coach one clean practice rep before adding load, speed, partner input, or decision demand.
execution_steps: Set stance, grip, and rib-pelvis position before moving the load.
Move through the planned range while the hips and trunk share the rotational or diagonal force demand.
Return under control and stop the set before load path, brace, or posture changes.
coach_cues: Rotate from the ground up.
Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Load the hips, not the spine.
Control the return as much as the drive.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
Load is progressed before the athlete controls the rotational path.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The rep counts only if the athlete keeps foot pressure, rib-pelvis organization, clean rotational path, safe finish, and a reset that could be repeated.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.

dosage.notes: 2-4 sets x 5-8 controlled reps each side where applicable.

scaling.youth_beginner: Teach the pattern of Cable Shot-Put Press with no load or light load, emphasizing stance, brace, and controlled rotation.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Keep Cable Shot-Put Press crisp and low dose. Add one constraint only after the athlete can repeat posture, breathing, and safe finish.
scaling.teen: Use Cable Shot-Put Press as part of a rotational development progression. Keep quality higher than volume and avoid fatigue-based twisting.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the lowest-risk version of Cable Shot-Put Press. Reduce load, range, lever length, tempo difficulty, and asymmetry before adding resistance.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress Cable Shot-Put Press with load, range, intent, or unilateral demand while keeping clean rib-pelvis alignment.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, smaller range, lower speed, and easier exits for Cable Shot-Put Press. Avoid dizziness, aggressive twisting, or fast direction changes unless already trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance when needed. Avoid breath-holding, abdominal pressure symptoms, pelvic symptoms, dizziness, aggressive trunk rotation, and positions that feel unsafe during Cable Shot-Put Press.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-specific adjustment. Scale by symptoms, training age, body size, rotation history, pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure symptoms, sport demand, and current readiness.
doNotUseWhen: The athlete has pain, dizziness, neurological symptoms, or medical restrictions that make rotation unsafe.
The athlete cannot control basic stance, brace, or landing positions for the chosen variation.
The session has already created enough fatigue that rotation quality or decision quality is degraded.

readiness_checks: Athlete can rotate through pain-free range without pinching or guarding.
Athlete can brace and breathe without breath holding or rib flare.
Athlete understands the start, finish, reset, and stop command.
contraindications: Current spine, hip, shoulder, knee, or ankle pain aggravated by rotation.
Uncontrolled dizziness, balance loss, or symptoms during turning.
Inability to follow spacing, throw, landing, or stop commands.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Med Ball Slam to Rotational Throw with a light-to-moderate medicine ball. Load the body through the feet and hips, rotate with a stacked trunk, release or catch cleanly, and reset before the next throw.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.

Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Fast intent, low volume.
Throw or move through the target, not through your low back.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
Too many reps turn power into conditioning.
Throw or cut accuracy drops but the set continues anyway.
Ball is too heavy, making the throw slow or arm-dominant.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The attempt counts only if speed or throw intent is high, the rotation is sequenced from the ground up, the landing/finish is controlled, and the athlete can reset without rushing.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.
Throw speed, jump/contact quality, cut angle, or acceleration intent drops noticeably.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Med Ball Rebound Rotational Catch-and-Throw with a light-to-moderate medicine ball. Load the body through the feet and hips, rotate with a stacked trunk, release or catch cleanly, and reset before the next throw.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.
Choose a medicine ball light enough to move fast without changing posture or release path.
Use a solid wall and stand far enough away that the ball rebound or release is safe.
Coach one clean practice rep before adding load, speed, partner input, or decision demand.
execution_steps: Start in the prescribed stance and create quiet tension through feet, hips, and trunk.
Load or reorient under control, then express the throw, jump, cut, or sprint with high intent.
Stick, catch, release, or finish cleanly; reset fully before the next attempt.
coach_cues: Rotate from the ground up.
Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Fast intent, low volume.
Throw or move through the target, not through your low back.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
Too many reps turn power into conditioning.
Throw or cut accuracy drops but the set continues anyway.
Ball is too heavy, making the throw slow or arm-dominant.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The attempt counts only if speed or throw intent is high, the rotation is sequenced from the ground up, the landing/finish is controlled, and the athlete can reset without rushing.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.
Throw speed, jump/contact quality, cut angle, or acceleration intent drops noticeably.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Reactive 45-Degree Cut over a short, clearly marked distance. The athlete rotates or reorients the hips and trunk, moves with intent, brakes or finishes under control, and resets before fatigue changes mechanics.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.
Mark the lane, cut angle, or finish target with cones so speed and distance are constrained.
Coach one clean practice rep before adding load, speed, partner input, or decision demand.
execution_steps: Start in the prescribed stance and create quiet tension through feet, hips, and trunk.
Load or reorient under control, then express the throw, jump, cut, or sprint with high intent.
Stick, catch, release, or finish cleanly; reset fully before the next attempt.
coach_cues: Rotate from the ground up.
Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Fast intent, low volume.
Throw or move through the target, not through your low back.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
Too many reps turn power into conditioning.
Throw or cut accuracy drops but the set continues anyway.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The attempt counts only if speed or throw intent is high, the rotation is sequenced from the ground up, the landing/finish is controlled, and the athlete can reset without rushing.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.
Throw speed, jump/contact quality, cut angle, or acceleration intent drops noticeably.

cards.name: Pivot-to-Sprint Start
cards.category: Rotational Speed, Elasticity, and Change-of-Direction Integration
cards.cardSummary: Pivot-to-Sprint Start is a Output exercise for pivot, reorient, and accelerate. Use it when the goal is rotational skill, force transfer, or power quality rather than fatigue chasing.
cards.bestPlacement: Use early in the main session after warm-up and movement prep, before heavy strength, long sport blocks, or conditioning. Rest fully between attempts.
cards.description: Perform Pivot-to-Sprint Start over a short, clearly marked distance. The athlete rotates or reorients the hips and trunk, moves with intent, brakes or finishes under control, and resets before fatigue changes mechanics.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: acceleration_start_speed. Coach Pivot-to-Sprint Start as rotational-athlete work: rotation should be organized through feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, shoulders, and the implement or direction change - not forced through the low back.
cards.athleteLanguage: Load clean, rotate fast, finish under control, then reset.
cards.selectionRationale: Finishes the library by connecting rotational body orientation to first-step projection and short acceleration.
cards.whyItWorks: Finishes the library by connecting rotational body orientation to first-step projection and short acceleration. Rotational power improves when the athlete can sequence force from the ground into the hips, trunk, shoulders, and implement or direction change. Low reps and full rest preserve speed, accuracy, and tissue safety.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Output because the main adaptation is freshness-sensitive rotational power, speed, jumping, throwing, cutting, or acceleration quality.
cards.commonMisuse: Using high-intent throws, jumps, cuts, or sprints as conditioning. Rotational power is lost when reps become slow, inaccurate, painful, or sloppy.
cards.scalingGuidance: Progress one variable at a time: implement load, speed, approach, angle, distance, cue uncertainty, or rebound demand. Regress to static stance, lighter ball, lower speed, or stick finish first.

rotational_power_profile.rotation_source: feet, hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders; avoid forced lumbar twisting
rotational_power_profile.sequence_priority: load or gather, brace, rotate, transfer, finish, and reset
rotational_power_profile.quality_rule: The attempt counts only if speed or throw intent is high, the rotation is sequenced from the ground up, the landing/finish is controlled, and the athlete can reset without rushing.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Pivot-to-Sprint Start over a short, clearly marked distance. The athlete rotates or reorients the hips and trunk, moves with intent, brakes or finishes under control, and resets before fatigue changes mechanics.
setup: Clear the training area and define the athlete's start position, finish position, and stop command.
Mark the lane, cut angle, or finish target with cones so speed and distance are constrained.
Coach one clean practice rep before adding load, speed, partner input, or decision demand.
execution_steps: Start in the prescribed stance and create quiet tension through feet, hips, and trunk.
Load or reorient under control, then express the throw, jump, cut, or sprint with high intent.
Stick, catch, release, or finish cleanly; reset fully before the next attempt.
coach_cues: Rotate from the ground up.
Ribs and pelvis travel as a team.
Own the finish before the next rep.
Fast intent, low volume.
Throw or move through the target, not through your low back.
common_faults: Low back becomes the main rotation point.
Feet lose pressure or the athlete spins on the toes without a controlled base.
Ribs flare, shoulders shrug, or pelvis dumps forward during the turn.
Too many reps turn power into conditioning.
Throw or cut accuracy drops but the set continues anyway.
breathing_cues: Exhale through effort or release.
Do not hold breath to force rotation.
Recover breathing before the next high-intent rep.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: The attempt counts only if speed or throw intent is high, the rotation is sequenced from the ground up, the landing/finish is controlled, and the athlete can reset without rushing.
stop_signs: Sharp pain, joint pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or symptom increase.
Lumbar spine becomes the main source of rotation instead of hips, pelvis, thoracic spine, and shoulders.
The athlete loses foot pressure, trunk stack, safe landing, throw accuracy, or controlled reset.
Throw speed, jump/contact quality, cut angle, or acceleration intent drops noticeably.

dosage.notes: 3-5 sets x 1-3 short accelerations/cuts; full recovery between attempts.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a lighter implement or bodyweight version of Pivot-to-Sprint Start, short distances, and a clear stop rule; coach accuracy and landing before power.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Keep Pivot-to-Sprint Start crisp and low dose. Add one constraint only after the athlete can repeat posture, breathing, and safe finish.
scaling.teen: Use Pivot-to-Sprint Start as part of a rotational development progression. Keep quality higher than volume and avoid fatigue-based twisting.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the lowest-risk version of Pivot-to-Sprint Start. Reduce speed, range, load, approach steps, decision demand, and total attempts before increasing intensity.
scaling.adult_advanced: Increase only one variable at a time for Pivot-to-Sprint Start: intent, approach speed, cut angle, implement load, or reactive cue complexity.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, smaller range, lower speed, and easier exits for Pivot-to-Sprint Start. Avoid dizziness, aggressive twisting, or fast direction changes unless already trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance when needed. Avoid breath-holding, abdominal pressure symptoms, pelvic symptoms, dizziness, aggressive trunk rotation, and positions that feel unsafe during Pivot-to-Sprint Start.
